stem: In a school performance study comparing School A vs. School B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher test score improvement (71.7% vs 51.7%). However, when stratified by socioeconomic background: In affluent area students (n_X=20, n_Y=100): Treatment X achieves 88% vs Treatment Y's 79%. In lower-income area students (n_X=100, n_Y=20): Treatment X achieves 44% vs Treatment Y's 36%. The true population proportion of affluent area students is uncertain, estimated between 38% and 51%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to lower-income area students (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.084, 0.085], point estimate: 0.084. Treatment X is genuinely better by 8.4 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=51.7%, Y=71.7% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In affluent area students: X=88% > Y=79%. In lower-income area students: X=44% > Y=36%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because School A is applied more to lower-income area students (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
Not considering that additional unobserved confounders could further modify the comparison
